metadata.name: declaredata_fuse
metadata.summary: Python client bindings for DeclareData Fuse server

functions.name: declaredata_fuse.session.session
documentation:
Context manager for configuring and creating a new session, executing an
action, then shutting it down (even if the action raised an exception).

Example usage:

with session("localhost", 8080, "myapp") as fuse:
    csv_dataframe = fuse.read.csv("s3://somebucket/somefile.csv")

classes.name: declaredata_fuse.session.FuseSessionBuilder

methods.name: declaredata_fuse.session.FuseSessionBuilder.appName
documentation:
Set the name of the application that should be associated with
the FuseSession that will be built, then return the same
FuseSessionBuilder instance.

methods.name: declaredata_fuse.session.FuseSessionBuilder.host
documentation:
Set the host to which the FuseSession will connect, after it's built,
then return the same FuseSessionBuilder instance.

methods.name: declaredata_fuse.session.FuseSessionBuilder.port
documentation:
Set the port to which the FuseSession will connect, after it's built,
then return the same FuseSessionBuilder instance.

methods.name: declaredata_fuse.session.FuseSessionBuilder.getOrCreate
documentation: Build the new FuseSession

A builder for FuseSessions. This is intended to be a singleton for
production code, so don't create instances of FuseSessionBuilder directly.
Instead, use FuseSession.builder and then call methods on that.

classes.name: declaredata_fuse.session.FuseDataSource

methods.name: declaredata_fuse.session.FuseDataSource.csv
documentation:
Load a CSV from the given filename into a new DataFrame on which
you can start doing operations.

Depending on how your Fuse server is configured, you may be able to
specify different file prefixes like `s3://` and `https://`.

Local filesystem filenames are always supported regardless of
configuration.

A data source from which to read DataFrames. Do not create an instance
of this class directly. Instead, create a new FuseSession (using the
FuseSession.builder builder) and then use FuseSession.read

classes.name: declaredata_fuse.session.FuseSession

methods.name: declaredata_fuse.session.FuseSession.read
documentation:
The FuseDataSource singleton with which to read data.

See documentation at the top of FuseSession for a usage example

methods.name: declaredata_fuse.session.FuseSession.get_builder
documentation:
Get a FuseSessionBuilder configured with the given app name,
host and port.

If app_name is unspecified or passed as None, uses a unique
app name.

methods.name: declaredata_fuse.session.FuseSession.sql
documentation:
Use the existing session to execute SQL and return a DataFrame
with the results.

methods.name: declaredata_fuse.session.FuseSession.stop
documentation:
Drop all DataFrames associated with this session and close
the connection to the server.

Important note:

This may be a _very destructive_ action, especially if you have
created a lot of dataframes (directly or indirectly) from this session.

A new session with the Fuse server, with which data can be read and
procesed. The general way to create a new session is as follows:

session = FuseSession.builder.appName("sampleapp").host("localhost").port(8080).getOrCreate()

After you have a session, you can use the 'read' property to start
reading data:

csv_dataframe = session.read.csv("s3://somebucket/somefile.csv")

See documentation in FuseDataSource for more information on session.read

classes.name: declaredata_fuse.functions.Function

methods.name: declaredata_fuse.functions.Function.alias
documentation:
Create a new function that will put the return value of the existing
function into a new column with the specified name

methods.name: declaredata_fuse.functions.Function.over
documentation:
Create a new function that will execute the existing function over the
given window

methods.name: declaredata_fuse.functions.Function.to_pb
documentation:
Convert this function into a protobuf-compatible structure.

This is not for public use.

A function that will be executed over the values of 1 or more columns
over a series of rows.

If you don't already have an instance of Function, the best way to create
a new one is with one of the free-standing functions like sum() or count()

If you do already have an instance of Function, you can create new,
derivative Functions from that one using methods like alias() and over()

classes.name: declaredata_fuse.dataframe.DataFrameWriter

methods.name: declaredata_fuse.dataframe.DataFrameWriter.saveAsTable
documentation:
Save the DataFrame to a table with the specified name.

After this function successfully succeeds, you can call reference the
new table name in FuseSession.sql, using the same FuseSession from
which this DataFrame originated.

A convenience class that knows how to write a DataFrame to a table
that can be queried from SQL.

Generally, instances of this class should be created by creating
a DataFrame, then calling DataFrame.write()

classes.name: declaredata_fuse.dataframe.DataFrame

methods.name: declaredata_fuse.dataframe.DataFrame.write
documentation:
Prepare this DataFrame to have its contents written to a table
so that it can be queried from SQL. See documentation under
DataFrameWriter for more details.

methods.name: declaredata_fuse.dataframe.DataFrame.take

Get the first num rows from this DataFrame and return them
as a list of Row

See the following pyspark documentation for more:

https://spark.apache.org/docs/latest/api/python/reference/pyspark.sql/api/pyspark.sql.DataFrame.take.html

methods.name: declaredata_fuse.dataframe.DataFrame.groupBy

Start building an aggregation on this DataFrame. Start by grouping

methods.name: declaredata_fuse.dataframe.DataFrame.__getattr__
documentation:
Convenience method for referencing a column in this DataFrame.

Allows you to do the following (assuming mydf is a DataFrame with
column mycol)

    mydf.mycol

See the following pyspark documentation for more:

https://spark.apache.org/docs/latest/api/python/reference/pyspark.sql/api/pyspark.sql.DataFrame.__getattr__.html

methods.name: declaredata_fuse.dataframe.DataFrame.__getitem__
documentation:
Convenience method for referencing a column in this DataFrame

Allows you to do the following (assuming mydf is a DataFrame with
column mycol)

    mydf["mycol"]

See the following pyspark documentation for more:

https://spark.apache.org/docs/latest/api/python/reference/pyspark.sql/api/pyspark.sql.DataFrame.__getitem__.html?highlight=__getitem__#pyspark.sql.DataFrame.__getitem__

methods.name: declaredata_fuse.dataframe.DataFrame.export_csv
documentation:
Eagerly evaluate the DataFrame, collect all its data, format it as
a CSV, and return the CSV contents as a str

methods.name: declaredata_fuse.dataframe.DataFrame.sort_typed
documentation:
Sort the rows in this DataFrame based on the values in the given column.

This is a strongly-typed method for sorting, not included in the
standard pyspark API. Use DataFrame.sort for the compatible version.

methods.name: declaredata_fuse.dataframe.DataFrame.sort
documentation:
Sort the rows in this DataFrame based on the given columns. Directions
can be set individually on each column, or globally with the ascending
keyword.

For more, see pyspark documentation:

https://spark.apache.org/docs/latest/api/python/reference/pyspark.sql/api/pyspark.sql.DataFrame.sort.html?highlight=sort#pyspark.sql.DataFrame.sort

methods.name: declaredata_fuse.dataframe.DataFrame.orderBy
documentation:
Alias for self.sort(*cols, **kwargs)

For more, see pyspark documentation:

https://spark.apache.org/docs/latest/api/python/reference/pyspark.sql/api/pyspark.sql.DataFrame.orderBy.html?highlight=orderby#pyspark.sql.DataFrame.orderBy

methods.name: declaredata_fuse.dataframe.DataFrame.filter
documentation:
Return a new DataFrame with rows filtered out from this DataFrame, using
the given filter condition.

methods.name: declaredata_fuse.dataframe.DataFrame.withColumn
documentation:
Return a new DataFrame with a single column added to it with the given
name. Values in the new column will be calculated by the given function.

class_variables.name: declaredata_fuse.dataframe.DataFrame.df_uid
documentation: The unique ID of this DataFrame. Do not modify

class_variables.name: declaredata_fuse.dataframe.DataFrame.stub
documentation: The connection to the Fuse server. Do not modify

The core class in the Fuse library. DataFrame represents tabular data that
has the following properties:

- It either came directly from a FuseSession (i.e. via a call to
    FuseSession.read.csv()), or came from an operation on an existing
    DataFrame
- It is "owned" by exactly one FuseSession - when its owner is destroyed
    with a call to FuseSession.stop, it (and all other DataFrames owned by
    that FuseSession) are immediately invalidated and deleted
- It is _lazy_ - most operations may return very quickly and will only be
    _evaluated_ when the data is actually requested. Eager (non-lazy)
    operations will be clearly documented.
- It it _immutable_ - all mutating operations will return a _new_
    DataFrame that reflects the changes.

methods.name: declaredata_fuse.proto.sds_pb2_grpc.sdsServicer.CloseSession
documentation:
//////////////////////////////////////
session destruction methods
//////////////////////////////////////

Close a session and free all associated dataframes.

This is a highly destructive method, because all dataframes
created directly or indirectly as part of this session
will be instantly deleted.

Modify this window spec to alter the "window frame". In other words,

class_variables.name: declaredata_fuse.window.WindowSpec.order_col
documentation:
The column whose values should be used to order the rows in 
the window.

If this is None, an arbitrary ordering, that is not guaranteed
and may change over time, will be chosen.

class_variables.name: declaredata_fuse.window.WindowSpec.partition_col
documentation:
The column whose values should be used to choose partitions prior to 
constructing windows.

If this is None, partitions will be chosen in an unspecified way that
may change over time.
The specification for a window query
